newbornBilirubin.name: Newborn Bilirubin Calculator
newbornBilirubin.description: Bhutani nomogram — estimate the risk of newborn jaundice (hyperbilirubinemia).

meta.title: Newborn Bilirubin Calculator — Bhutani Nomogram | Health Calculators
meta.description: Estimate newborn jaundice risk using the Bhutani nomogram. Hour-specific percentiles, mg/dL ⇄ µmol/L. For term and near-term infants ≥ 35 weeks gestation.

newbornBilirubin.title: Newborn Bilirubin Calculator
newbornBilirubin.description: Assess the risk of newborn jaundice using the Bhutani hour-specific bilirubin nomogram — based on postnatal age in hours and total serum bilirubin.
newbornBilirubin.incompleteHint: Enter postnatal age (12 – 168 hours) and total bilirubin to see the risk zone.

hints.lowRisk: TSB below the 40th percentile. Significant hyperbilirubinemia is unlikely. Continue routine follow-up as advised by your midwife or pediatrician.
hints.lowIntermediate: TSB between the 40th and 75th percentile. Risk is low but not negligible — a follow-up bilirubin check within the next 24–48 hours is reasonable.
hints.highIntermediate: TSB between the 75th and 95th percentile. Increased risk of significant hyperbilirubinemia. Close follow-up, evaluate risk factors, and discuss AAP treatment thresholds with your pediatrician.
hints.highRisk: TSB ≥ 95th percentile. High risk. Seek prompt medical evaluation. Phototherapy may be indicated depending on risk factors and AAP thresholds.

newbornBilirubin.howItWorksText: The Bhutani nomogram (Pediatrics 1999) maps a newborn's total serum bilirubin to an hour-specific percentile. Four risk zones are defined by the 40th, 75th, and 95th percentiles of TSB values from healthy term and near-term infants. Applies to infants ≥ 35 weeks gestation and ≥ 2000 g — not to preterm or sick newborns. Treatment thresholds follow the AAP 2022 guideline.
newbornBilirubin.disclaimer: This estimate is not a diagnosis. Treatment decisions (e.g. phototherapy) belong to the pediatrician or neonatologist. Risk factors such as hemolysis, late-preterm birth, or sepsis lower the action thresholds.

faq.a: Newborn jaundice (icterus neonatorum) results from elevated bilirubin in the blood. Mild yellowing of the skin in the first days of life is common and usually harmless — but very high values can damage the brain (kernicterus). Timely risk assessment is therefore important.

faq.q: How does the Bhutani nomogram work?
faq.a: It compares the infant's total serum bilirubin (TSB) to age-specific percentiles derived from a large cohort of healthy term newborns. A value above the 95th percentile indicates a high risk of significant hyperbilirubinemia.

faq.q: Which babies is this calculator for?
faq.a: Term and near-term newborns ≥ 35 weeks gestation and ≥ 2000 g, between 12 and 168 hours of age (0.5 – 7 days). It is not appropriate for preterm or sick infants — they require individualised thresholds.

faq.q: What are risk factors for severe jaundice?
faq.a: Major risk factors include hemolysis (e.g. blood-group incompatibility, G6PD deficiency), lower gestational age (35–36 weeks), feeding difficulties with weight loss, cephalohematoma, a sibling who needed phototherapy, and East Asian ancestry. These lower the treatment thresholds.

Indications follow the AAP 2022 guideline: thresholds depend on postnatal age, gestational age, and risk factors. Only the pediatrician decides — this calculator provides orientation, not a treatment decision.

The gold standard is total serum bilirubin (TSB) from venous or capillary blood. Transcutaneous bilirubinometers (TcB) screen on the skin — values ≥ 14 mg/dL or those close to action thresholds are confirmed by serum measurement.
Both units describe the same concentration. Conversion factor: 1 mg/dL ≈ 17.1 µmol/L. The calculator converts automatically — enter the unit your laboratory report uses.
